title: Notification Delivery And Preferences And Channels Semantic Contract
description: Source-backed draft semantic contract for the notification delivery and preferences and channels capability, recovered from historical source-lineage evidence as lineage. Facets cite verified source-import manifest rows; proof is hermetic/runtime lineage, not fresh USF proof.
aiGuidance: Source-backed draft. Facet descriptions are concise source-backed summaries citing verified manifest rows; proof is hermetic/stale, not current readiness. Enrich facet detail and publish fresh proof before generation; respect the hexagonal constraint in ADR 0005.

lifecycle.description: The lifecycle is: a user sets channel and category preferences, a test or domain notification is dispatched, disabled channels are suppressed, enabled channels are delivered through the local, email, or webhook transport, each attempt is logged as sent|suppressed|failed, and channel readiness is reported. Notifications are requested by workflow and other producer events only after preference and channel checks pass, and a missing recipient fails rather than being faked as sent. Transport-provider failure degrades readiness and never marks an undelivered notification as delivered.

stateModel.description: State comprises preferences keyed by organisation, user, channel, and category with an enabled flag; a notification log with status sent|suppressed|failed; channel transports of email|webhook|local; resolved recipients; and per-channel readiness availability. This capability holds tenant data, so its records participate in tenant backup, export, retention, and legal-hold semantics. Recipient resolution and preference state are tenant-scoped and audit metadata deliberately excludes secret-bearing payloads.

permissions.description: Self-service preference reads and writes use profile.read_self and profile.update_self with tenant context derived from the request host, while operator readiness and test-send routes are global and require platform.notifications.read or platform.notifications.write. A user can therefore manage only their own preferences, and operator test sends are gated behind operator permissions. Tenant isolation must be preserved because the capability holds tenant-visible data.

contracts.description: The contract surface is GET and PATCH /api/me/notification-preferences, GET /api/admin/notifications/readiness, and POST /api/admin/tenants/:tenantId/notifications/test, fronting a NotificationTransportPort with email and webhook transport adapters. Producer capabilities such as the workflow engine request notifications through the shared event contract rather than calling transports directly. The port seam keeps each transport provider swappable behind a uniform dispatch contract.

validation.description: Invalid profile or preference bodies are rejected, an invalid tenant id is rejected, secret-bearing notification payloads are rejected before dispatch, disabled preferences suppress delivery, and a missing recipient fails rather than reporting a fake send. These invariants are exercised by the notification preference, dispatch, and transport runtime proofs so suppression and failure are explicit states, not silent drops.

errorModel.description: Outcomes are deterministic and fail closed: states include sent, suppressed, failed, readiness unavailable or degraded, no-tenant, validation error, invalid tenant id, and transport non-2xx failure, with each surfaced explicitly. Degraded mode disables the transport-dependent mutation and side-effect paths while keeping read-only readiness and audit visible. No failure path fabricates a successful send or echoes secret payload fields.

auditModel.description: Preference changes and operator test sends are audit-before-change or audit-before-action operations, so the audit record precedes the effect. Dispatch records a durable per-attempt status (sent, suppressed, or failed), and the audit metadata deliberately excludes secret-bearing payload content. This separates auditable operator and user actions from the durable delivery log.

readinessModel.description: Readiness reports the real availability of the local, SMTP, and webhook transports; the Mailpit and webhook proofs skip honestly when their backing services are absent and never mark a fake send as sent. Because this is a tenant-data capability, readiness must also preserve tenant isolation and audit signals, and a missing isolation or audit signal is an alert condition. Provider failure degrades readiness explicitly rather than upgrading a composed or production-shaped state to production-live.

proof.description: Behaviour is exercised by the notification-preferences, notification-dispatch, notification-email-transport, notification-webhook-transport, and notification-transport-routes runtime proofs, described in the corpus as level-4 live Postgres, Mailpit, and webhook route validation. The proofs are the lineage evidence, not a USF readiness claim. Proof is hermetic/runtime lineage; fresh USF proof pending USF-101/USF-59.

uiSemanticDefinition.description: No verified UI source path in the manifest yet; author the UI semantic model before generation.
